*EH1210.1*
February 18, 2026
ENGROSSED
HOUSE BILL No. 1210
_____
DIGEST OF HB 1210 (Updated February 17, 2026 10:56 am - DI 129)
Citations Affected: Numerous provisions throughout the Indiana
Code.
Synopsis: Department of local government finance. Requires a
municipal entity that hires or retains a municipal advisor to complete
a request for qualifications at least once every three years to select the
municipal advisor. Requires a municipal advisor to register with the
department of local government finance (DLGF). Expires certain
municipal entity contracts. Modifies the amount of supplemental
wagering tax that the treasurer of state is required to pay to the
riverboat operating in Gary and the distribution of wagering tax
revenue to the city of Gary. Provides that the Lake County Convention
Center Authority is established upon the substantial completion of the
convention and event center. Establishes an alternative procedure for
(Continued next page)
Effective: Upon passage; July 1, 2018 (retroactive); January 1, 2024
(retroactive); January 1, 2025 (retroactive); May 10, 2025 (retroactive);
July 1, 2025 (retroactive); January 1, 2026 (retroactive); July 1, 2026;
July 1, 2027; January 1, 2028; June 30, 2028; July 1, 2028; January 1,
2029.
Snow, Lopez, Slager, Pryor
(SENATE SPONSORS — HOLDMAN, BALDWIN, MISHLER, ROGERS,
RANDOLPH LONNIE M)
January 5, 2026, read first time and referred to Committee on Ways and Means.
January 27, 2026, amended, reported — Do Pass.
January 29, 2026, read second time, amended, ordered engrossed.
January 30, 2026, engrossed.
February 2, 2026, read third time, passed. Yeas 82, nays 1.
SENATE ACTION
February 5, 2026, read first time and referred to Committee on Tax and Fiscal Policy.
February 17, 2026, amended, reported favorably — Do Pass.
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

Digest Continued
certain school corporations to issue refunding bonds. Requires state
agencies and political subdivisions to cooperate with the state GIS
officer in preparing a statewide base map. Eliminates the requirement
that the DLGF work with the office of technology or another
organization that is part of a state educational institution for purposes
of posting information on the Indiana transparency website and
submitting forms regarding data for local units. Allows a person to
satisfy any notice statute by publishing notice in specified forms of
media and modifies related provisions regarding notice by publication.
Provides that in assessing or reassessing land, the land shall be
assessed as agricultural land regardless of who owns the property or
who is liable for the property taxes. Specifies the burden of proof in
particular circumstances involving land previously assessed as
agricultural land. Repeals provisions regarding the assessment of
certain industrial facilities. Modifies procedures as to the reporting of
assessment values of real and personal property and parcel level data.
Changes the deadline for a county to submit to the DLGF data
regarding real property, personal property, and geographic information
system information. Requires the purchaser of a mobile home to
process the paperwork with the bureau of motor vehicles to transfer the
title into the purchaser's name within 90 days of the sale. Specifies
eligibility for certain townships to petition for an increase to the
maximum property tax levy for the firefighting and emergency services
fund. Makes procedural changes for civil taxing units not subject to
levy limits. Provides that certain local property tax credits result in a
reduction of property tax collections in a political subdivision in which
such a credit is applied. Specifies the procedures for the submission of
certain forms and related allocation amounts with regard to various
allocation areas. Amends various tax increment financing allocation
area provisions, including provisions to redefine "residential property"
and to require the original owner of certain nonowner-occupied
residential property in an allocation area to enter into a written
agreement with the appropriate entity to pay the property taxes for the
portion of outstanding bonds until the bonds are retired. Changes
reporting requirements by governing bodies to the DLGF regarding
guaranteed savings contracts and energy efficient programs used by
school corporations. Provides that the property tax rate for the levy
imposed for the replacement of fire protection territory equipment is
considered part of the maximum permissible ad valorem property tax
levy and may not exceed $0.0333 per $100 of assessed value. Extends
a temporary increase in the capitalization rate percentage under the
statewide agricultural land base rate determination. Requires the DLGF
to annually publish on the Indiana Register the adjusted cost estimate
threshold for certain local public work projects. Provides a real and
personal property tax exemption for Indiana nonprofit senior living
communities beginning with property taxes that are first due and
payable in 2027. Adds the the Indiana Historical Society, Inc. to a list
of organizations exempt from property taxation. Allows certain
taxpayers to retroactively file a property tax exemption application.
Increases the number of model residences and residences in inventory
from three to seven that may be claimed for purposes of those property
tax deductions. Requires an individual to reside on the real property,
mobile home, or manufactured home to be eligible for the over 65
property tax credit. Increases the property tax deduction for a veteran
who is totally disabled to 100% of the assessed value of the individual's
real property (instead of $14,000). Expires property tax deductions for
certain veterans, and instead provides a property tax liability credit.
Restores the property tax deduction available to a surviving spouse of
a World War I veteran that was limited by SEA 1-2025. Provides, if a
taxpayer claims the homestead deduction for property that is not
eligible for the deduction, that the taxpayer shall (instead of may) be
liable for any additional taxes that would have been due on homestead
(Continued next page)
EEHH 11221100——LLSS 66880055//DDII 113344

Digest Continued
property plus a civil penalty. Requires the county auditor to include in
a notice of tax due a 10% fine as a penalty for claiming the homestead
deduction falsely, which is in addition to all other penalties for which
the taxpayer is liable. Allows the executive of Miami Township in Cass
County to submit a petition to the DLGF requesting an increase in the
township's maximum permissible ad valorem property tax levy for
property taxes first due and payable in 2027. Requires the Hancock
County fiscal body to adopt a resolution to allow a one time transfer of
money from the library property tax replacement fund. Provides that for
purposes of fixing and reviewing budgets, tax rates, and tax levies,
before a county auditor makes an amendment, the county auditor must
provide written notice to the county fiscal body, the DLGF, and the
fiscal officers of the affected taxing units. Provides that the DLGF may
not approve the budget for a political subdivision until an attestation
statement concerning the uploading of contracts is submitted. Requires
certain qualified data center users to enter into an agreement with local
officials before the qualified data center user may use a specific
transaction award certificate. Adds a provision regarding repayment in
the redevelopment tax credit. Specifies eligibility and procedures for
a health reimbursement arrangement income tax credit. Moves the
effective date for the local income tax changes enacted in SEA 1-2025
from 2028 to 2029. Makes corresponding changes to move the
expiration date regarding a county with a single voting bloc enacted in
HEA 1142-2025. Specifies procedures for the imposition of local
income taxes and distribution of local income tax revenue. Provides
that, for counties or municipalities that fail to adopt an ordinance to
renew an existing expenditure tax rate, the expenditure tax rate for the
county or municipality shall be the minimum tax rate necessary for
existing debt service. Provides that the county may determine an
allocation method for revenue raised from a tax rate for fire protection
or emergency medical services. Provides that the county and certain
township fire departments must receive an allocation of revenue raised
from a tax rate for fire protection or emergency medical services.
Provides a formula for the distribution of revenue from the local
income tax rate imposed by a county for certain small cities and towns.
Specifies procedures for determining population for purposes of a
municipal local income tax rate. Allows each county to establish a local
strategic taskforce to negotiate and determine certain maximum local
income tax rates. Allows the Marshall County jail fund to be used for
costs otherwise incurred for the operation of the county jail. Provides
that distributions of certain excise tax revenue to a taxing unit may be
deposited in any fund maintained by the taxing unit and may be used
for any purpose allowed by law. Makes numerous changes and
additions to the local innkeeper's tax and food and beverage tax
statutes, including new food and beverage tax authorizations,
modifications to distributions of innkeeper's tax revenue, and revisions
to the composition of various innkeeper's tax boards and commissions.
Replaces the definitions of "manufactured home" and "mobile home"
throughout the Indiana Code with a singular definition. Limits voting
eligibilty in homeowners associations to members of the homeowners
association who use their property as a homestead. Subject to an
exception, prohibits a unit from adopting or enforcing an ordinance,
resolution, regulation, policy, or rule that prohibits or restricts an owner
of a privately owned residential property from using the property as a
rental property. Retroactively amends the definition of "short term
rental" to specify that certain private, owner occupied businesses are
excluded from that definition. Requires a person appointed to a fire
protection district board of trustees to reside in the fire protection
district. Sets forth an alternative procedure that may be used to appoint
the board of trustees of certain fire protection districts. Provides that a
fire protection district established after December 31, 2025, may not
impose a tax rate within the district that exceeds $0.40 per $100 of
(Continued next page)
EEHH 11221100——LLSS 66880055//DDII 113344

Digest Continued
assessed valuation. Revises a provision of the municipal Barrett Law
concerning deferred installments. Amends provisions in the drainage
law as to bidding on certain projects and the term of loans. Authorizes
the budget agency, subject to budget committee review, to augment the
state agency contingency fund appropriations in HEA 1001-2025
through July 1, 2027, in an amount not to exceed $40,000,000 for
Indiana office of technology contracts, in addition to the uses for the
appropriation authorized in HEA 1001-2025.
EEHH 11221100——LLSS 66880055//DDII 113344

February 18, 2026
Second Regular Session of the 124th General Assembly (2026)
PRINTING CODE. Amendments: Whenever an existing statute (or a section of the Indiana
Constitution) is being amended, the text of the existing provision will appear in this style type,
additions will appear in this style type, and deletions will appear in this style type.
Additions: Whenever a new statutory provision is being enacted (or a new constitutional
provision adopted), the text of the new provision will appear in this style type. Also, the
word NEW will appear in that style type in the introductory clause of each SECTION that adds
a new provision to the Indiana Code or the Indiana Constitution.
Conflict reconciliation: Text in a statute in this style type or this style type reconciles conflicts
between statutes enacted by the 2025 Regular Session of the General Assembly.

and local administration.

4 proposed precinct establishment order would comply with this chapter,

11 executive.

15 (1) The name of each existing precinct whose boundaries would

2 SECTION 9, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
3 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 8. (a) As used in this section, "audit threshold
4 number" refers to the following number:
5 (1) One (1), if the total number of votes cast, as determined under
6 subsection (c), is not more than twenty (20).
7 (2) Two (2), if the total number of votes cast, as determined under
8 subsection (c), is:
9 (A) more than twenty (20); but
10 (B) not more than forty (40).
11 (3) Three (3), if the total number of votes cast, as determined
12 under subsection (c), is:
13 (A) more than forty (40); but
14 (B) not more than sixty (60).
15 (4) Four (4), if the total number of votes cast, as determined under
16 subsection (c), is:
17 (A) more than sixty (60); but
18 (B) not more than eighty (80).
19 (5) Five percent (5%) of the total number of votes cast, rounded
20 up to the nearest whole number, if the total number of votes cast,
21 as determined under subsection (c), is:
22 (A) more than eighty (80); but
23 (B) not more than five hundred (500).
24 (6) Twenty-five (25), if the total number of votes cast, as
25 determined under subsection (c) is more than five hundred (500).
26 (b) As used in this section, "judge" refers only to the judge who is
27 a member of a political party other than the political party of the
28 inspector.

35 according to the poll lists for each precinct;
36 to determine if the total number of votes cast on the electronic voting

18 SECTION 6. IC 4-23-7.3-5.5 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA
19 CODE AS A NEW SECTION TO READ AS FOLLOWS
20 [EFFECTIVE JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 5.5. As used in this chapter,
21 "governmental boundary units" includes:
22 (1) the geographic boundaries of a political subdivision;
23 (2) the geographic boundaries of a taxing district (as defined
24 by IC 6-1.1-1-20); and
25 (3) any geographic boundaries related to the operation of the
26 statewide 911 system under IC 36-8-16.7.

30 officer, through the data center, the IGIC, and the other organizations,

36 under IC 4-13.1-2-2(a)(6) by the office of technology and through

38 access through the IGIC and other entities as directed by the state
39 GIS officer.

8
1 shall cooperate and participate as requested by the state GIS

3 (4) Maintain a state historical archive of GIS data, framework

8 (6) Provide assistance to state agencies and political subdivisions

19 Indiana Business Research Center.

13 SECTION 14. IC 5-1-5-2, AS AMENDED BY P.L.229-2011,
14 SECTION 62, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
15 UPON PASSAGE]: Sec. 2. (a) The governing body of any issuing body
16 may by ordinance provide for the issuance of bonds to refund
17 outstanding bonds issued at any time by such issuing body or its
18 predecessor, and to pay redemption premiums and costs of refunding
19 to effect a saving to the issuing body. Issuance of bonds to refund
20 outstanding bonds may also be made in order to pay or discharge all or
21 any part of such outstanding series or issue of bond, including any
22 interest thereon, in arrears or about to become due and for which
23 sufficient funds are not available or to modify restrictive covenants in
24 outstanding bonds impeding additional financing. To determine
25 whether or not a savings will be effected, consideration shall be given
26 to the estimated or known interest payable to the fixed maturities of the
27 refunding bonds, the interest payable on the bonds to be refunded, the
28 costs of issuance of the refunding bonds, including any sale discount,
29 the redemption premiums, if any, to be paid, and the probable earned
30 income from the investment of the refunding bond proceeds pending
31 redemption of the bonds to be refunded.
32 (b) The provisions of subsection (a) requiring a savings to be
33 effected do not apply to:
34 (1) the issuance of bonds to refund previously issued refunding
35 bonds, if the statute under which the refunding bonds are issued
36 expressly exempts such an issue from this savings requirement;
37 or
38 (2) the issuance of refunding bonds by a school corporation that
39 is an eligible school corporation under section 2.5 or 2.6 of this
40 chapter.
41 SECTION 15. IC 5-1-5-2.6 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA CODE
42 AS A NEW SECTION TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

28
1 UPON PASSAGE]: Sec. 2.6. (a) This section applies as an
2 alternative to section 2.5 of this chapter.
3 (b) As used in this section, "eligible school corporation" means
4 a school corporation (as defined in IC 36-1-2-17) that satisfies all
5 the conditions required by this section.
6 (c) As used in this section, "increment" means the annual
7 difference between:
8 (1) the annual debt service payment for the bonds proposed to
9 be retired or refunded; and
10 (2) the annual debt service payment for the proposed
11 refunding bonds;
12 for each year that the bonds that are being retired or refunded
13 would have been outstanding.
14 (d) In order for a school corporation to be an eligible school
15 corporation under this section, the school corporation must
16 determine that:
17 (1) the percentage computed under subsection (e) for the
18 school corporation is at least sixty percent (60%); and
19 (2) application of this section will not reduce revenue to other
20 local units in the same county;
21 regarding the year for which the latest certified levies have been
22 determined.
23 (e) A school corporation shall compute its percentage as follows:
24 (1) Compute the amount of credits granted under
25 IC 6-1.1-20.6 against the school corporation's levy for the
26 school corporation's operations fund (IC 20-46-8).
27 (2) Compute the school corporation's levy for the school
28 corporation's operations fund.
29 (3) Divide the amount computed under subdivision (1) by the
30 amount computed under subdivision (2) and express it as a
31 percentage.
32 (f) A school corporation that desires to be an eligible school
33 corporation under this section must submit a written request for
34 a certification by the department of local government finance that
35 the computation of the school corporation's percentage computed
36 under subsection (d)(1) and school corporation's determination
37 under subsection (d)(2) are correct. The department of local
38 government finance shall, not later than thirty (30) business days
39 after the date the department receives the school corporation's
40 request, make its determination and, if correct, certify that the
41 percentage computed under subsection (d)(1) and the
42 determination under subsection (d)(2) for the school corporation
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

29
1 meets the definition of an eligible school corporation.
2 (g) A school corporation that desires to be an eligible school
3 corporation under this section must, and notwithstanding any
4 other law, adopt a resolution that sets forth the following:
5 (1) The determinations made under subsection (d)(1) and
6 (d)(2), including the department of local government finance's
7 certification of the percentage computed under subsection (e)
8 and determination under subsection (d)(2).
9 (2) A determination providing for the:
10 (A) issuance of bonds to refund bonds or leases issued by
11 or on behalf of the school corporation before July 1, 2025;
12 and
13 (B) payment of redemption premiums and the costs of the
14 refunding.
15 (3) With respect to the refunding bonds, the following:
16 (A) The maximum principal amount.
17 (B) The maximum interest rate.
18 (C) The annual lease or debt service payment.
19 (D) The final maturity date.
20 (E) The estimated amount of the increment that will occur
21 for each year that the bonds that are being retired or
22 refunded by the issuance of refunding bonds would have
23 been outstanding.
24 (F) A finding that the annual debt service or lease payment
25 on the refunding bonds will not increase the annual debt
26 service or lease payment above the annual debt service or
27 lease payment approved by the school corporation for the
28 original project.
29 If the governing body adopts a resolution under this section, the
30 governing body must publish notice of the adoption of the
31 resolution as required by IC 5-3-1.
32 (h) An eligible school corporation may issue refunding bonds as
33 permitted by this section. In addition, an eligible school
34 corporation may extend the repayment period beyond the
35 repayment period for the bonds that are being retired or refunded
36 by the issuance of refunding bonds. However, the repayment
37 period may be extended only once for a particular bond, and the
38 extension may not exceed ten (10) years after the latest maturity
39 date for any of the bonds being retired or refunded by the eligible
40 school corporation under this section.

10 SECTION 19. IC 5-1-14-19 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA CODE
11 AS A NEW SECTION TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE JULY
12 1, 2026]: Sec. 19. (a) This section applies to a contract between a
13 municipal entity and a municipal adviser entered into, renewed, or
14 amended after June 30, 2026.
15 (b) As used in this section, "municipal advisor" means an entity
16 defined as a municipal advisor under Section 15B of the Securities
17 Exchange Act.
18 (c) A municipal advisor must meet the following criteria:
19 (1) A municipal advisor employing more than one (1)
20 employee must employ at least one (1) individual who meets
21 both of the following criteria:
22 (A) The individual is registered as a municipal advisor
23 under applicable federal law and regulations.
24 (B) The individual has successfully passed the Municipal
25 Advisor Representative Qualification Examination (Series
26 54), or any successor examination recognized by the
27 Municipal Securities Rulemaking Board.
28 (2) A municipal advisor employing only one (1) employee
29 must employ an individual who meets both of the following
30 criteria:
31 (A) The individual is registered as a municipal advisor
32 under applicable federal law and regulations.
33 (B) The individual has successfully passed the Municipal
34 Advisor Representative Qualification Examination (Series
35 50), or any successor examination recognized by the
36 Municipal Securities Rulemaking Board.
37 (d) As used in this section, "municipal entity" refers to:
38 (1) a state agency (as defined in IC 4-1-10-2);
39 (2) a county;
40 (3) a township;
41 (4) a city;
42 (5) a town;
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

33
1 (6) a school corporation;
2 (7) a special taxing district;
3 (8) an instrumentality of an entity listed in subdivisions (1)
4 through (7); and
5 (9) any other entity required to sell bonds pursuant to
6 IC 5-1-11.
7 (e) An entity that provides municipal advisory services,
8 including but not limited to financial planning, debt issuance, and
9 budget management advisory services, to:
10 (1) a state agency;
11 (2) a political subdivision (as defined in IC 36-1-2-13); or
12 (3) a municipal entity located in Indiana;
13 must comply with the municipal advisor qualification requirements
14 under subsection (c).
15 (f) If a municipal entity hires or retains a municipal advisor, the
16 municipal entity shall complete a request for qualifications at least
17 once every three (3) years to select the municipal advisor. The
18 request for qualifications must include a scope of services and an
19 evaluation criteria outline. The request for qualifications shall be
20 completed before July 1 each year it is completed.
21 (g) Municipal entity contracts in place longer than three (3)
22 years as of July 1, 2026, expire on July 1, 2026.
23 (h) The municipal entity shall publish a contract entered into
24 with a municipal advisor in a prominent location on the municipal
25 entity's website and on the department of local government
26 finance's computer gateway.
27 (i) Once selected for a municipal entity's contract, the municipal
28 advisor shall register with the department of local government
29 finance. The department of local government finance shall give the
30 municipal advisor access to the municipal entity's gateway portal
31 upon receipt of their registration. If the municipal advisor does not
32 register with the department of local government finance, the
33 department of local government finance may issue a fine of ten
34 thousand dollars ($10,000) to the municipal advisor.

13 the board, the governor shall fill the vacancy by appointing a new
14 director. The successor of each such director is appointed for a term of
15 four (4) years, except that any person appointed to fill a vacancy is
16 appointed to serve only for the unexpired term and until a successor is
17 duly appointed and qualified. A director is eligible for reappointment.
18 (d) The directors shall hold an initial organizational meeting within
19 thirty (30) days after the board's appointment and after public notice
20 given by the budget director in accordance with IC 5-3-1-4.
21 IC 5-3-1-1.5. As soon as practicable after January 15 of each year, the
22 board shall hold its annual organizational meeting. The board shall
23 elect one (1) of the directors as vice chair and another director as
24 secretary-treasurer to perform the duties of those offices. These officers
25 serve from the date of their election and until their successors are
26 elected and qualified. Special meetings may be called by the chair or
27 any two (2) directors of the board.
28 (e) Three (3) directors constitute a quorum of the board, and the
29 affirmative vote of at least three (3) directors is necessary for any
30 official action taken by the board. A vacancy in the membership of the
31 board does not impair the rights of a quorum to exercise all the rights
32 and perform all the duties of the board.
33 (f) Except for the budget director, the directors are entitled to
34 reimbursement for traveling expenses and other expenses actually
35 incurred in connection with their duties as provided by law. Directors
36 are not entitled to the salary per diem provided by IC 4-10-11-2.1(b) or
37 any other compensation while performing their duties.
38 (g) All expenses incurred in carrying out the provisions of this
39 chapter shall be payable solely from funds provided under this chapter
40 or from the proceeds of bonds issued by the authority under this
41 chapter, and no liability or obligation shall be incurred by the
42 commission or the authority under this chapter beyond the extent to
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

35
1 which money shall have been provided under the authority of this
2 chapter.
3 (h) The board:
4 (1) is responsible for implementing the powers and duties of the
5 commission under this chapter;
6 (2) may adopt bylaws for the regulation of the affairs of the board,
7 the conduct of the business of the commission, and the
8 safeguarding of the funds and property entrusted to the
9 commission; and
10 (3) shall, without complying with IC 4-22-2, adopt the code of
11 ethics specified in executive order 05-12 for its members and
12 employees.

29 SECTION 25. IC 5-3-1-4 IS REPEALED [EFFECTIVE JULY 1,
30 2026]. Sec. 4. (a) Whenever officers of a political subdivision are
31 required to publish a notice affecting the political subdivision, they
32 shall publish the notice in two (2) newspapers published in the political
33 subdivision.
34 (b) This subsection applies to notices published by county officers.
35 If there is only one (1) newspaper published in the county, then
36 publication in that newspaper alone is sufficient.
37 (c) This subsection applies to notices published by city, town, or
38 school corporation officers. If there is only one (1) newspaper
39 published in the municipality or school corporation, then publication
40 in that newspaper alone is sufficient. If no newspaper is published in
41 the municipality or school corporation, then publication of the notice
42 shall be made in one (1) of the following:
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

42
1 (1) A locality newspaper that circulates within the municipality or
2 school corporation.
3 (2) A newspaper published in the county in which the
4 municipality or school corporation is located and that circulates
5 within the municipality or school corporation.
6 (d) This subsection applies to notices published by officers of
7 political subdivisions not covered by subsection (a) or (b). If there is
8 only one (1) newspaper published in the political subdivision, then the
9 notice shall be published in that newspaper. If no newspaper is
10 published in the political subdivision, then publication of the notice
11 shall be made in one (1) of the following:
12 (1) A locality newspaper that circulates within the municipality or
13 school corporation.
14 (2) A newspaper published in the county and that circulates
15 within the political subdivision.
16 (e) This subsection applies to a political subdivision, including a
17 city, town, or school corporation. Notwithstanding any other law, if a
18 political subdivision has territory in more than one (1) county, public
19 notices that are required by law or ordered to be published must be
20 given as follows:
21 (1) By publication in two (2) newspapers published within the
22 boundaries of the political subdivision.
23 (2) If only one (1) newspaper is published within the boundaries
24 of the political subdivision, by publication of the notice in that
25 newspaper and in one (1) of the following:
26 (A) A locality newspaper that circulates within the political
27 subdivision.
28 (B) In another newspaper:
29 (i) published in any county in which the political subdivision
30 extends; and
31 (ii) that has a general circulation in the political subdivision.
32 (3) If no newspaper is published within the boundaries of the
33 political subdivision, by publishing the notice in two (2)
34 publications, consisting of either or both of the following:
35 (A) A locality newspaper that circulates within the political
36 subdivision.
37 (B) A newspaper that:
38 (i) is published in any counties into which the political
39 subdivision extends; and
40 (ii) has a general circulation in the political subdivision.
41 (4) If only one (1) newspaper is published in any of the counties
42 into which the political subdivision extends, by publication of the
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

43
1 notice in one (1) of the following:
2 (A) A locality newspaper that circulates within the political
3 subdivision.
4 (B) The newspaper published in the county if the newspaper
5 circulates within the political subdivision.
6 (f) A political subdivision may, in its discretion, publish public
7 notices in a qualified publication or additional newspapers or locality
8 newspapers to provide supplementary notification to the public. The
9 cost of publishing supplementary notification is a proper expenditure
10 of the political subdivision.

1 statute in which ad valorem property taxes are allocated, including the

16 assessed value of each tax increment financing allocation area to
17 neutralize the effect of the changing tax rates resulting year to year
18 from the homestead deduction under IC 6-1.1-12-37(c)(2) and
19 IC 6-1.1-12-37.5(c) and the deduction for eligible property under
20 IC 6-1.1-12-47. It is the intent of the general assembly that an increase
21 in revenue from a change in tax rates resulting from these statutes
22 accrue only to the base assessed value and not to the tax increment
23 financing allocation area. However, in the case of a decrease in revenue
24 from a change in tax rates resulting from these statutes, the department
25 may neutralize the change under this subsection in a positive manner
26 with regard to the tax increment financing allocation area to protect the
27 ability to pay bonds based on incremental revenue, if the tax increment
28 financing allocation area demonstrates to the department that an
29 adjustment is needed before the department calculates a positive
30 neutralization adjustment.

48
1 determined for the preceding assessment date, a capitalization

12 determined for the preceding assessment date, a capitalization

29 (d) Except as provided in subsection (f), for the:
30 (1) January 1, 2017, January 1, 2018, and January 1, 2019,
31 assessment dates, the assessed value limit for purposes of
32 subsection (c) is one hundred seventy-five thousand dollars
33 ($175,000);
34 (2) January 1, 2020, January 1, 2021, January 1, 2022, and
35 January 1, 2023, assessment dates, the assessed value limit for
36 purposes of subsection (c) is two hundred thousand dollars
37 ($200,000); and
38 (3) January 1, 2024, assessment date and for each assessment date
39 thereafter, the assessed value limit for purposes of subsection (c)
40 is two hundred forty thousand dollars ($240,000).
41 (e) (c) An individual who has sold real property, a mobile home not
42 assessed as real property, or a manufactured home not assessed as real
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

59
1 property to another person under a contract that provides that the
2 contract buyer is to pay the property taxes on the real property, mobile
3 home, or manufactured home may not claim the deduction provided
4 under this section against that real property, mobile home, or
5 manufactured home.
6 (d) Beginning with taxes assessed in 2026 and due and payable
7 in 2027, an individual who receives a deduction under this section
8 may not receive a local property tax credit under IC 6-1.1-51.3.
9 (f) For purposes of determining the assessed value of the real
10 property, mobile home, or manufactured home under subsection (d) for
11 an individual who has received a deduction under this section in a
12 previous year, increases in assessed value that occur after the later of:
13 (1) December 31, 2019; or
14 (2) the first year that the individual has received the deduction;
15 are not considered unless the increase in assessed value is attributable
16 to substantial renovation or new improvements. Where there is an
17 increase in assessed value for purposes of the deduction under this
18 section, the assessor shall provide a report to the county auditor
19 describing the substantial renovation or new improvements, if any, that
20 were made to the property prior to the increase in assessed value.
21 SECTION 50. IC 6-1.1-12-14.5, AS AMENDED BY P.L.230-2025,
22 SECTION 33, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
23 JANUARY 1, 2026 (RETROACTIVE)]: Sec. 14.5. (a) As used in this
24 section, "homestead" has the meaning set forth in section 37 of this
25 chapter.
26 (b) An individual may claim a deduction from the assessed value of
27 the individual's homestead if:
28 (1) the individual served in the military or naval forces of the
29 United States for at least ninety (90) days;
30 (2) the individual received an honorable discharge;
31 (3) the individual has a disability of at least fifty percent (50%);
32 (4) the individual's disability is evidenced by:
33 (A) a pension certificate or an award of compensation issued
34 by the United States Department of Veterans Affairs; or
35 (B) a certificate of eligibility issued to the individual by the
36 Indiana department of veterans' affairs after the Indiana
37 department of veterans' affairs has determined that the
38 individual's disability qualifies the individual to receive a
39 deduction under this section; and
40 (5) the homestead was conveyed without charge to the individual
41 who is the owner of the homestead by an organization that is
42 exempt from income taxation under the federal Internal Revenue
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

60
1 Code.
2 (c) If an individual is entitled to a deduction from assessed value
3 under subsection (b) for the individual's homestead, the amount of the
4 deduction is determined as follows:
5 (1) If the individual is totally disabled, the deduction is equal to
6 one hundred percent (100%) of the assessed value of the
7 homestead.
8 (2) If the individual has a disability of at least ninety percent
9 (90%) but the individual is not totally disabled, the deduction is
10 equal to ninety percent (90%) of the assessed value of the
11 homestead.
12 (3) If the individual has a disability of at least eighty percent
13 (80%) but less than ninety percent (90%), the deduction is equal
14 to eighty percent (80%) of the assessed value of the homestead.
15 (4) If the individual has a disability of at least seventy percent
16 (70%) but less than eighty percent (80%), the deduction is equal
17 to seventy percent (70%) of the assessed value of the homestead.
18 (5) If the individual has a disability of at least sixty percent (60%)
19 but less than seventy percent (70%), the deduction is equal to
20 sixty percent (60%) of the assessed value of the homestead.
21 (6) If the individual has a disability of at least fifty percent (50%)
22 but less than sixty percent (60%), the deduction is equal to fifty
23 percent (50%) of the assessed value of the homestead.
24 (d) An individual who claims a deduction under this section for an
25 assessment date may not also claim a deduction under section 13
26 (before its expiration) or 14 of this chapter for that same assessment
27 date.
28 (e) An individual who desires to claim the deduction under this
29 section must claim the deduction in the manner specified by the
30 department of local government finance.

32 year and any succeeding year for which the deduction is allowed.
33 (f) To obtain the deduction for a desired calendar year under this
34 section in which property taxes are first due and payable, the individual
35 desiring to claim the deduction must do the following as applicable:
36 (1) Complete, date, and file the certified statement described in
37 subsection (e) on or before January 15 of the calendar year in
38 which the property taxes are first due and payable.
39 (2) Satisfy any recording requirements on or before January 15 of
40 the calendar year in which the property taxes are first due and
41 payable for a homestead described in subsection (a)(2).
42 (g) Except as provided in subsection (l), if a person who is
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

71
1 receiving, or seeks to receive, the deduction provided by this section in
2 the person's name:
3 (1) changes the use of the individual's property so that part or all
4 of the property no longer qualifies for the deduction under this
5 section; or
6 (2) is not eligible for a deduction under this section because the
7 person is already receiving:
8 (A) a deduction under this section in the person's name as an
9 individual or a spouse; or
10 (B) a deduction under the law of another state that is
11 equivalent to the deduction provided by this section;
12 the person must file a certified statement with the auditor of the county,
13 notifying the auditor of the person's ineligibility, not more than sixty

18 person had filed the statement as required by this subsection plus a
19 civil penalty equal to ten percent (10%) of the additional taxes due. The
20 civil penalty imposed under this subsection is in addition to any interest
21 and penalties for a delinquent payment that might otherwise be due.
22 One percent (1%) of the total civil penalty collected under this
23 subsection shall be transferred by the county to the department of local
24 government finance for use by the department in establishing and
25 maintaining the homestead property data base under subsection (j) and,
26 to the extent there is money remaining, for any other purposes of the
27 department. This amount becomes part of the property tax liability for
28 purposes of this article.
29 (h) The department of local government finance may adopt rules or
30 guidelines concerning the application for a deduction under this
31 section.
32 (i) This subsection does not apply to property in the first year for

1 married couple a deduction under this section if:

9 required from the homestead owner under subsection (e)(4) for the sole

12 IC 6-3.6-5 (before its expiration). Each county auditor shall submit data
13 on deductions applicable to the current tax year on or before March 15
14 of each year in a manner prescribed by the department of local
15 government finance.
16 (k) A county auditor may require an individual to provide evidence
17 proving that the individual's residence is the individual's principal place
18 of residence as claimed in the certified statement filed under subsection
19 (e). The county auditor may limit the evidence that an individual is
20 required to submit to a state income tax return, a valid driver's license,
21 or a valid voter registration card showing that the residence for which
22 the deduction is claimed is the individual's principal place of residence.
23 The county auditor may not deny an application filed under section 44
24 of this chapter because the applicant does not have a valid driver's
25 license or state identification card with the address of the homestead
26 property. The department of local government finance shall work with
27 county auditors to develop procedures to determine whether a property
28 owner that is claiming a standard deduction or homestead credit is not
29 eligible for the standard deduction or homestead credit because the
30 property owner's principal place of residence is outside Indiana.
31 (l) A county auditor shall grant an individual a deduction under this
32 section regardless of whether the individual and the individual's spouse
33 claim a deduction on two (2) different applications and each

35 owned by the individual's spouse is located outside Indiana and the
36 individual files an affidavit with the county auditor containing the
37 following information:
38 (1) The names of the county and state in which the individual's

40 allowed by this section.
41 (2) A statement made under penalty of perjury that the following
42 are true:
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

73
1 (A) That the individual and the individual's spouse maintain
2 separate principal places of residence.

5 residence.

7 for that same year, claimed a standard or substantially similar
8 deduction for any property other than the property maintained
9 as a principal place of residence by the respective individuals.
10 A county auditor may require an individual or an individual's spouse to

16 information.

38 assessment date occurs; or

41 assessment date occurs;
42 (2) on the assessment date:
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

74
1 (A) the property on which the homestead is currently located
2 was vacant land; or
3 (B) the construction of the dwelling that constitutes the
4 homestead was not completed; and
5 (3) either:
6 (A) the individual files the certified statement required by
7 subsection (e); or
8 (B) a sales disclosure form that meets the requirements of
9 section 44 of this chapter is submitted to the county assessor
10 on or before December 31 of the calendar year for the
11 individual's purchase of the homestead.
12 An individual who satisfies the requirements of subdivisions (1)
13 through (3) is entitled to the deduction under this section for the
14 homestead for the assessment date, even if on the assessment date the
15 property on which the homestead is currently located was vacant land
16 or the construction of the dwelling that constitutes the homestead was
17 not completed. The county auditor shall apply the deduction for the
18 assessment date and for the assessment date in any later year in which
19 the homestead remains eligible for the deduction. A homestead that
20 qualifies for the deduction under this section as provided in this
21 subsection is considered a homestead for purposes of section 37.5 of
22 this chapter and IC 6-1.1-20.6.
23 (o) This subsection applies to an application for the deduction
24 provided by this section that is filed for an assessment date occurring
25 after December 31, 2013. Notwithstanding any other provision of this
26 section, an individual buying a mobile home that is not assessed as real
27 property or a manufactured home that is not assessed as real property
28 under a contract providing that the individual is to pay the property
29 taxes on the mobile home or manufactured home is not entitled to the
30 deduction provided by this section unless the parties to the contract
31 comply with IC 9-17-6-17.
32 (p) This subsection:
33 (1) applies to an application for the deduction provided by this
34 section that is filed for an assessment date occurring after
35 December 31, 2013; and
36 (2) does not apply to an individual described in subsection (o).
37 The owner of a mobile home that is not assessed as real property or a
38 manufactured home that is not assessed as real property must attach a
39 copy of the owner's title to the mobile home or manufactured home to
40 the application for the deduction provided by this section.
41 (q) For assessment dates after 2013, the term "homestead" includes
42 property that is owned by an individual who:
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

75
1 (1) is serving on active duty in any branch of the armed forces of
2 the United States;

4 (3) was otherwise eligible, without regard to this subsection, for
5 the deduction under this section for the property for the
6 assessment date immediately preceding the transfer date specified
7 in the order described in subdivision (2).
8 For property to qualify under this subsection for the deduction provided

16 Notwithstanding subsection (a)(2), the property remains a homestead

23 Otherwise, the property ceases to qualify as a homestead under this
24 subsection if the property is leased while the individual is away from
25 Indiana. Property that qualifies as a homestead under this subsection
26 shall also be construed as a homestead for purposes of section 37.5 of
27 this chapter.

29 satisfies each of the following requirements:
30 (1) The property is located in Indiana and consists of a dwelling
31 and includes up to one (1) acre of land immediately surrounding
32 that dwelling, and any of the following improvements:
33 (A) Any number of decks, patios, gazebos, or pools.
34 (B) One (1) additional building that is not part of the dwelling
35 if the building is predominately used for a residential purpose
36 and is not used as an investment property or as a rental
37 property.
38 (C) One (1) additional residential yard structure other than a
39 deck, patio, gazebo, or pool.
40 (2) The property is the principal place of residence of an
41 individual.
42 (3) The property is owned by an entity that is not described in

1 subsection (a)(2)(B).

1 (1) a user fee (as defined in IC 33-23-1-10.5);
2 (2) any other charge, fee, or rate imposed by a political
3 subdivision under any other law; or
4 (3) any tax imposed by a political subdivision other than a
5 property tax.
6 (i) This subsection applies only to an appeal based on a claim of
7 error in the determination of property that is or is not eligible for a
8 standard homestead deduction under IC 6-1.1-12-37 and only for an
9 assessment date occurring before January 1, 2024. A taxpayer may
10 appeal an error in the assessment of property as described in this
11 subsection any time after the official's action, but not later than one (1)
12 year after the date on which the property that is the subject of the
13 appeal was assessed.
14 (j) In an appeal of the assessed value of a property:
15 (1) the county or township assessor in conjunction with the
16 preliminary informal meeting; or
17 (2) the county board in conjunction with an appeal hearing by
18 the county board;
19 may request a taxpayer to provide income data necessary to
20 determine the assessed value under the income capitalization
21 approach. If requested, a taxpayer shall provide income data
22 within sixty (60) days of the request.

1 SECTION 171, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS
2 [EFFECTIVE JANUARY 1, 2026 (RETROACTIVE)]: Sec. 9.8. (a)
3 This section applies to property taxes first due and payable after
4 December 31, 2009.
5 (b) The following definitions apply throughout this section:
6 (1) "Debt service obligations of a political subdivision" refers to:
7 (A) the principal and interest payable during a calendar year
8 on bonds; and
9 (B) lease rental payments payable during a calendar year on
10 leases;
11 of a political subdivision payable from ad valorem property taxes.
12 (2) "Protected taxes" refers to the following:
13 (A) Property taxes that are exempted from the application of
14 a credit granted under section 7 or 7.5 of this chapter by
15 section 7(b), 7(c), 7.5(b), or 7.5(c) of this chapter or another
16 law.
17 (B) Property taxes imposed by a political subdivision to pay
18 for debt service obligations of a political subdivision that are
19 not exempted from the application of a credit granted under
20 section 7 or 7.5 of this chapter by section 7(b), 7(c), 7.5(b), or
21 7.5(c) of this chapter or any other law. Property taxes
22 described in this clause are subject to the credit granted under
23 section 7 or 7.5 of this chapter by section 7(b), 7(c), 7.5(b), or
24 7.5(c) of this chapter regardless of their designation as
25 protected taxes.
26 (3) "Unprotected taxes" refers to property taxes that are not
27 protected taxes.
28 (c) Except as provided in section 9.9 of this chapter, the total
29 amount of revenue to be distributed to the fund for which the protected
30 taxes were imposed shall be determined as if no credit were granted
31 under section 7, or 7.5, or 7.7 of this chapter or under IC 6-1.1-49.
32 The total amount of the loss in revenue resulting from the granting of
33 credits under section 7, or 7.5, or 7.7 of this chapter or under
34 IC 6-1.1-49 must reduce only the amount of unprotected taxes
35 distributed to a fund using the following criteria:
36 (1) The reduction may be allocated in the amounts determined by
37 the political subdivision using a combination of unprotected taxes
38 of the political subdivision in those taxing districts in which the
39 credit caused a reduction in protected taxes.
40 (2) The tax revenue and each fund of any other political
41 subdivisions must not be affected by the reduction.
42 (d) When:
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

99
1 (1) the revenue that otherwise would be distributed to a fund
2 receiving only unprotected taxes is reduced entirely under
3 subsection (c) and the remaining revenue is insufficient for a fund
4 receiving protected taxes to receive the revenue specified by
5 subsection (c); or
6 (2) there is not a fund receiving only unprotected taxes from
7 which to distribute revenue;
8 the revenue distributed to the fund receiving protected taxes must also
9 be reduced. If the revenue distributed to a fund receiving protected
10 taxes is reduced, the political subdivision may transfer money from one
11 (1) or more of the other funds of the political subdivision to offset the
12 loss in revenue to the fund receiving protected taxes. The transfer is
13 limited to the amount necessary for the fund receiving protected taxes
14 to receive the revenue specified under subsection (c). The amount
15 transferred shall be specifically identified as a debt service obligation
16 transfer for each affected fund.

42 (1) to refinance or renew prior bond or lease rental obligations
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

100
1 existing before January 1, 2024, but only if the refinancing or
2 renewal is for a lower interest rate; or
3 (2) indebtedness that is approved in a local public question or
4 referendum under IC 6-1.1-20 or any other law.
5 (c) Subject to subsection (a) (before January 1, 2024) and
6 subsection (b) (after December 31, 2023), a school corporation is
7 eligible to allocate credits proportionately under this section for 2019,
8 2020, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024, 2025, or 2026 if the school corporation's
9 percentage computed under this subsection is at least ten percent (10%)
10 for its operations fund levy as certified by the department of local
11 government finance. A school corporation shall compute its percentage
12 under this subsection as determined under the following formula:
13 STEP ONE: Determine the amount of credits granted under this
14 chapter against the school corporation's levy for the school
15 corporation's operations fund.
16 STEP TWO: Determine the amount of the school corporation's
17 levy that is attributable to new debt incurred after June 30, 2019,
18 but is not attributable to the debt service levy described in
19 subsection (a)(1)(B) (before January 1, 2024) or subsection (b)(2)
20 (after December 31, 2023).
21 STEP THREE: Determine the result of the school corporation's
22 total levy minus any referendum levy.
23 STEP FOUR: Subtract the STEP TWO amount from the STEP
24 THREE amount.
25 STEP FIVE: Divide the STEP FOUR amount by the STEP
26 THREE amount expressed as a percentage.
27 STEP SIX: Multiply the STEP ONE amount by the STEP FIVE
28 percentage.
29 STEP SEVEN: Determine the school corporation's levy for the
30 school corporation's operations fund.
31 STEP EIGHT: Divide the STEP SIX amount by the STEP SEVEN
32 amount expressed as a percentage.
33 The computation must be made by taking into account the requirements
34 of section 9.8 of this chapter regarding protected taxes and the impact
35 of credits granted under this chapter on the revenue to be distributed to
36 the school corporation's operations fund for the particular year.
37 (d) A school corporation that desires to be an eligible school
38 corporation under this section must, before May 1 of the year for which
39 it wants a determination, submit a written request for a certification by
40 the department of local government finance that the computation of the
41 school corporation's percentage under subsection (c) is correct. The
42 department of local government finance shall, not later than June 1 of
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

101
1 that year, determine whether the percentage computed by the school
2 corporation under subsection (c) is accurate and certify whether the
3 school corporation is eligible under this section.
4 (e) For a school corporation that is certified as eligible under this
5 section, the school corporation may allocate the effect of the credits
6 granted under this chapter and IC 6-1.1-49 proportionately among all
7 the school corporation's property tax funds that are not exempt under
8 section 7.5(b) or 7.5(c) of this chapter, based on the levy for each fund
9 and without taking into account the requirements of section 9.8 of this
10 chapter regarding protected taxes as determined under the following
11 formula:
12 STEP ONE: Determine the product of:
13 (A) the percentage determined under STEP EIGHT of
14 subsection (c); multiplied by
15 (B) five (5).
16 STEP TWO: Determine the lesser of the STEP ONE percentage
17 or one hundred percent (100%).
18 STEP THREE: Determine the product of:
19 (A) the amount determined under STEP SIX of subsection (c);
20 multiplied by
21 (B) the STEP TWO percentage.
22 The school corporation may allocate the amount of credits determined
23 under STEP THREE proportionately under this section. The
24 department of local government finance shall include in its certification
25 of an eligible school corporation under subsection (d) the amount of
26 credits that the school corporation may allocate proportionately as
27 determined under this subsection.
28 (f) This section expires January 1, 2027.

16 SECTION 94. IC 6-1.1-51.3-1, AS ADDED BY P.L.68-2025,
17 SECTION 84, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
18 JANUARY 1, 2026 (RETROACTIVE)]: Sec. 1. (a) An individual is

34 (A) owns the real property, mobile home, or manufactured
35 home; or
36 (B) is buying the real property, mobile home, or manufactured

38 on the date the credit is claimed; and

EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

118
1 dollars ($60,000);

15 for the calendar year preceding by two (2) years the calendar year
16 in which the property taxes are first due and payable; and

18 or manufactured home.
19 (b) The amount of the credit is equal to one hundred fifty dollars
20 ($150).
21 (c) An individual may not be denied the credit provided under this
22 section because the individual is absent from the real property, mobile
23 home, or manufactured home while in a nursing home or hospital.

119
1 section against that real property.
2 (g) If individuals share ownership or are purchasing the property
3 under a contract as joint tenants or tenants in common and all of the
4 tenants are not at least sixty-five (65) years of age, the credit allowed
5 under this section shall be reduced by an amount equal to the credit
6 multiplied by a fraction. The numerator of the fraction is the number of
7 tenants who are not at least sixty-five (65) years of age, and the
8 denominator is the total number of tenants.
9 (h) An individual wishing to claim a credit under this section must
10 file a statement, on forms prescribed by the department of local
11 government finance, with the county auditor and provide
12 documentation necessary to substantiate the individual's eligibility for
13 the credit. The statement must be completed and dated on or before
14 January 15 of the calendar year in which the property taxes are first due
15 and payable. The statement may be filed in person or by mail. If
16 mailed, the mailing must be postmarked on or before the last day for
17 filing. An individual who remains eligible for the credit in the
18 following year is not required to file a statement to apply for the credit
19 in the following year. However, an individual who receives a credit
20 under this section in a particular year and who becomes ineligible for
21 the credit in the following year shall notify the auditor of the county in
22 which the homestead is located of the individual's ineligibility not later
23 than sixty (60) days after the individual becomes ineligible.

1 credit under this section; and

6 manufactured home under contract;
7 on the date the credit is claimed, and in the case of clause (B),
8 the contract or a memorandum of the contract is recorded in
9 the county recorder's office.
10 (b) The amount of the credit is equal to two hundred fifty
11 dollars ($250).
12 (c) The surviving spouse of an individual may receive the credit
13 provided by this section if:
14 (1) the individual satisfied the requirements of subsection
15 (a)(1) through (a)(4) at the time of death; or
16 (2) the individual:
17 (A) was killed in action;
18 (B) died while serving on active duty in the military or
19 naval forces of the United States; or
20 (C) died while performing inactive duty training in the
21 military or naval forces of the United States;
22 and the surviving spouse satisfies the requirement of subsection
23 (a)(5) at the time the credit is claimed. The surviving spouse is
24 entitled to the credit regardless of whether the property for which
25 the credit is claimed was owned by the deceased veteran or the
26 surviving spouse before the deceased veteran's death.
27 (d) An individual who receives the credit provided by this
28 section may receive any other property tax credit that the
29 individual is entitled to by law.

9 SECTION 98. IC 6-2.5-5-29 IS AMENDED TO READ AS
10 FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE UPON PASSAGE]: Sec. 29. (a) As used in
11 this section:
12 "Manufactured home" means a manufactured home as that term is
13 defined in 42 U.S.C. 5402(6) as that statute was adopted and in effect
14 on January 1, 1988. has the definition set forth in IC 9-13-2-96(a).
15 The term includes a mobile home (as defined in IC 9-13-2-103.2).
16 "Industrialized residential structure" means a structure that is both
17 an industrialized building system (as defined in IC 22-12-1-14) and a
18 one (1) or two (2) family private residence.
19 (b) Sales of manufactured homes or industrialized residential
20 structures are exempt from the state gross retail tax to the extent that
21 the gross retail income from the sales is not attributable to the cost of
22 materials used in manufacturing the manufactured home or
23 industrialized residential structure.
24 (c) For purposes of this section, the part of the gross retail income
25 not attributable to the cost of materials used in manufacturing a
26 manufactured home or an industrialized residential structure is
27 thirty-five percent (35%) of the gross retail income derived from the
28 sale of the manufactured home or industrialized residential structure.
29 (d) The gross retail income derived from the sale of a preowned
30 manufactured home is exempt from the state gross retail tax.

33 procedures to be used by the adopting body or governmental entity for
34 submitting to the department the notice, the adopting ordinance or
35 resolution, and the vote results on an ordinance or resolution. The
36 department of local government finance shall notify the submitting
37 entity within thirty (30) days after submission whether the department
38 has received the necessary information required by the department. A
39 final action taken by an adopting body or governmental entity under
40 this article to impose a new tax or amend an existing tax is not effective
41 until the department of local government finance notifies the adopting
42 body or governmental entity that it has received the required
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

131
1 information from the submitting entity.
2 (e) Not later than July 1 of each calendar year, the county
3 auditor shall certify to the department of local government finance
4 and to the state GIS officer which taxing units comprise each
5 taxing district in the county.

11 on bonds;
12 (2) lease rental payments payable during a calendar year on
13 leases; and

15 leases to be deposited in a sinking fund or other reserve
16 during a calendar year;
17 of a county, city, or town payable from local income taxes.

20 government finance with the total amount of the county's, city's, or
21 town's debt service obligations payable from local income tax

23 request by the department of local governing finance, any
24 additional ensuing calendar years.

4 SECTION 117. IC 6-3.6-3-6, AS AMENDED BY P.L.223-2025,
5 SECTION 6, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
6 UPON PASSAGE]: Sec. 6. (a) This section applies to a county in
7 which the county adopting body is a local income tax council.
8 (b) In the case of a city or town that lies within more than one (1)
9 county, the county auditor of each county shall base the allocations
10 required by subsections (d) and (e) on the population of that part of the
11 city or town that lies within the county for which the allocations are
12 being made.
13 (c) Each local income tax council has a total of one hundred (100)
14 votes.
15 (d) Each county, city, or town that is a member of a local income tax
16 council is allocated a percentage of the total one hundred (100) votes
17 that may be cast. The percentage that a city or town is allocated for a
18 year equals the same percentage that the population of the city or town
19 bears to the population of the county. The percentage that the county
20 is allocated for a year equals the same percentage that the population
21 of all areas in the county not located in a city or town bears to the
22 population of the county.
23 (e) This subsection applies only to a county with a single voting
24 bloc. Each individual who sits on the fiscal body of a county, city, or
25 town that is a member of the local income tax council is allocated for
26 a year the number of votes equal to the total number of votes allocated
27 to the particular county, city, or town under subsection (d) divided by
28 the number of members on the fiscal body of the county, city, or town.
29 This subsection expires May 31, 2027. 2028.
30 (f) On or before January 1 of each year, the county auditor shall
31 certify to each member of the local income tax council the number of
32 votes, rounded to the nearest one hundredth (0.01), each member has
33 for that year.
34 (g) This subsection applies only to a county with a single voting
35 bloc. On or before January 1 of each year, in addition to the
36 certification to each member of the local income tax council under
37 subsection (f), the county auditor shall certify to each individual who
38 sits on the fiscal body of each county, city, or town that is a member of
39 the local income tax council the number of votes, rounded to the
40 nearest one hundredth (0.01), each individual has under subsection (e)
41 for that year. This subsection expires May 31, 2027. 2028.

23 maximum aggregate tax rate allowable pursuant to IC 6-3.6-6-2,

32 and December 31 of a calendar year. Not later than thirty (30) days

37 (1) Any statutory requirements for an ordinance that
38 otherwise apply to an ordinance adopted under this article to
39 impose a local income tax rate also apply to an ordinance
40 adopted under this subsection.
41 (2) If the tax rate or tax rates adopted in an ordinance

EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

139
1 allowable tax rate or maximum allowable aggregate tax rate,

7 (b).

18 IC 6-3.6-7, the tax rate or tax rates that exceed the maximum
19 allowable rate shall be reduced to the maximum allowable
20 rate without further action by the adopting body.

22 IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(1) through IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(3) exceed the
23 maximum allowable aggregate rate in IC 6-3.6-6-2(c), the tax
24 rates shall be reduced without any further action by the

27 maximum allowable rate shall first be applied by reducing
28 the tax rate imposed under IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(1), but may not
29 reduce the rate below the tax rate otherwise required
30 under this article.
31 (B) Any remaining portion of the aggregate tax rate that
32 exceeds the maximum allowable rate after the reduction in
33 clause (A) shall be applied to reduce the tax rates imposed
34 under IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(2) and IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(3) in
35 proportion to the total rates imposed under

3 rate for the county under subsection (b)(1) or (b)(4) for a calendar
4 year shall be the minimum tax rate necessary to produce one and
5 twenty-five hundredths (1.25) times the sum of the:
6 (1) highest annual outstanding debt service;
7 (2) highest annual lease payments; and
8 (3) any amount required under the agreements for the bonds
9 or leases to be deposited in a sinking fund or other reserve;
10 for the calendar year payable from the applicable component rate.

1 (d) The amount of the tax revenue that is from the local income tax
2 rate imposed under this section and that is collected for a calendar year

18 necessary to produce one and twenty-five hundredths (1.25) times

24 for the year. This subsection applies regardless of whether there is a
25 modification in the tax rate or the rate is unchanged from the previous
26 year.

9 (repealed) for the school corporation's general fund. The county auditor

17 one (1) or more taxing units in the county, the county auditor shall
18 include adjustments to the current taxing unit's apportionment and
19 distributions, if necessary, so that the apportionment and distributions
20 accurately reflect the merger or consolidation of the taxing units.
21 However, for purposes of determining distributions under this section
22 for 2009 and each year thereafter, a state welfare and tuition support
23 allocation shall be deducted from the total amount available for
24 apportionment and distribution to taxing units under this section before

26 remit the state welfare and tuition support allocation to the treasurer of
27 state for deposit, as directed by the budget agency. The amount of the
28 state welfare and tuition support allocation for a county for a particular
29 year is equal to the result determined under STEP FOUR of the
30 following formula:
31 STEP ONE: Determine the result of the following:
32 (A) Separately for 1997, 1998, and 1999 for each taxing
33 district in the county, determine the result of:
34 (i) the amount appropriated in the year by the county from
35 the county's county welfare fund and county welfare
36 administration fund; divided by
37 (ii) the total amounts appropriated by all taxing units in the
38 county for the same year.
39 (B) Determine the sum of the clause (A) amounts.
40 (C) Divide the clause (B) amount by three (3).
41 (D) Determine the result of:
42 (i) the amount of excise taxes allocated to the taxing district
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

171
1 that would otherwise be available for distribution to taxing
2 units in the taxing district; multiplied by
3 (ii) the clause (C) amount.
4 STEP TWO: Determine the result of the following:
5 (A) Separately for 2006, 2007, and 2008 for each taxing
6 district in the county, determine the result of:
7 (i) the tax rate imposed in the taxing district for the county's
8 county medical assistance to wards fund, family and
9 children's fund, children's psychiatric residential treatment
10 services fund, county hospital care for the indigent fund,
11 children with special health care needs county fund, plus, in
12 the case of Marion County, the tax rate imposed by the
13 health and hospital corporation that was necessary to raise
14 thirty-five million dollars ($35,000,000) from all taxing
15 districts in the county; divided by
16 (ii) the aggregate tax rate imposed in the taxing district for
17 the same year.
18 (B) Determine the sum of the clause (A) amounts.
19 (C) Divide the clause (B) amount by three (3).
20 (D) Determine the result of:
21 (i) the amount of excise taxes allocated to the taxing district
22 that would otherwise be available for distribution to taxing
23 units in the taxing district after subtracting the STEP ONE
24 (D) amount for the same taxing district; multiplied by
25 (ii) the clause (C) amount.
26 (E) Determine the sum of the clause (D) amounts for all taxing
27 districts in the county.

30 district in the county, determine the result of:

32 district plus the tax rate imposed by the school corporation
33 for the school corporation's special education preschool fund
34 in the district; divided by
35 (ii) the aggregate tax rate imposed in the taxing district for
36 the same year.
37 (B) Determine the sum of the clause (A) amounts.
38 (C) Divide the clause (B) amount by three (3).
39 (D) Determine the result of:
40 (i) the amount of excise taxes allocated to the taxing district
41 that would otherwise be available for distribution to taxing
42 units in the taxing district after subtracting the STEP ONE
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

172
1 (D) amount for the same taxing district; multiplied by
2 (ii) the clause (C) amount.
3 (E) Determine the sum of the clause (D) amounts for all taxing
4 districts in the county.
5 STEP FOUR: Determine the sum of the STEP ONE, STEP TWO,
6 and STEP THREE amounts for the county.
7 If the boundaries of a taxing district change after the years for which a
8 ratio is calculated under STEP ONE, STEP TWO, or STEP THREE,
9 the state comptroller shall establish a ratio for the new taxing district
10 that reflects the tax rates imposed in the predecessor taxing districts. If
11 a new taxing district is established after the years for which a ratio is
12 calculated under STEP ONE, STEP TWO, or STEP THREE, the state
13 comptroller shall establish a ratio for the new taxing district and adjust
14 the ratio for other taxing districts in the county.
15 (d) Such determination shall be made from copies of vehicle
16 registration forms furnished by the bureau of motor vehicles. Prior to
17 such determination, the county assessor of each county shall, from
18 copies of registration forms, cause information pertaining to legal
19 residence of persons owning taxable vehicles to be verified from the
20 assessor's records, to the extent such verification can be so made. The

22 several taxing units within which such persons reside.

25 the assessor shall certify such information to the county auditor for the
26 auditor's use as soon as it is checked and completed.
27 (f) The taxing unit may deposit distributions received under this
28 chapter in any fund maintained by the taxing unit, and the
29 distributions may be used for any purpose allowed by law.

38 used to offset the person's property taxes or vehicle excise taxes, times

14 ratio is calculated under STEP ONE or STEP TWO, the state
15 comptroller shall establish a ratio for the new taxing district that
16 reflects the tax rates imposed in the predecessor taxing districts. If a
17 new taxing district is established after the years for which a ratio is
18 calculated under STEP ONE, STEP TWO, or STEP THREE, the state
19 comptroller shall establish a ratio for the new taxing district and adjust
20 the ratio for other taxing districts in the county.
21 (e) Within thirty (30) days following the receipt of excise taxes from
22 the department, the county treasurer shall file a report with the county
23 auditor concerning the aircraft excise taxes collected by the county
24 treasurer. The county treasurer shall file the report on the form
25 prescribed by the state board of accounts. The county treasurer shall,
26 in the manner and at the times prescribed in IC 6-1.1-27, make a
27 settlement with the county auditor for the aircraft excise taxes collected
28 by the county treasurer. The county treasurer shall, in the manner
29 prescribed by the state board of accounts, maintain records concerning
30 the aircraft excise taxes received and distributed by the treasurer.
31 (f) The taxing unit may deposit distributions received under this
32 chapter in any fund maintained by the taxing unit, and the
33 distributions may be used for any purpose allowed by law.

18 STEP THREE of the following formula:
19 STEP ONE: Determine the result of the following:
20 (A) Separately for 2006, 2007, and 2008 for each taxing
21 district in the county, determine the result of:
22 (i) the tax rate imposed in the taxing district for the county's
23 county medical assistance to wards fund, family and
24 children's fund, children's psychiatric residential treatment

29 thirty-five million dollars ($35,000,000) from all taxing
30 districts in the county; divided by
31 (ii) the aggregate tax rate imposed in the taxing district for
32 the same year.
33 (B) Determine the sum of the clause (A) amounts.
34 (C) Divide the clause (B) amount by three (3).
35 (D) Determine the result of:
36 (i) the amount of excise taxes allocated to the taxing district
37 that would otherwise be available for distribution to taxing
38 units in the taxing district; multiplied by
39 (ii) the clause (C) amount.
40 (E) Determine the sum of the clause (D) amounts for all taxing
41 districts in the county.
42 STEP TWO: Determine the result of the following:
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

179
1 (A) Separately for 2006, 2007, and 2008 for each taxing
2 district in the county, determine the result of:
3 (i) the tuition support levy tax rate imposed in the taxing
4 district plus the tax rate imposed by the school corporation
5 for the school corporation's special education preschool fund
6 in the district; divided by
7 (ii) the aggregate tax rate imposed in the taxing district for
